project.name: Weekly Commit Module
project.tagline: A micro-frontend that ties each week's work to company strategy
project.summary_plain: The Weekly Commit Module replaces the weekly-planning part of 15Five for Solovis. Each week an employee drafts a list of things they plan to do, and every item must be linked to a piece of company strategy before the week can be locked. After the week, they reconcile what they planned against what actually happened and carry unfinished work into next week. Managers review each person's week and see a team roll-up of completion and strategy alignment, and locking a week can also drop a calendar event into Outlook.
project.summary_deep: WCM is a client-side SPA micro-frontend (one Module-Federation remote, `wc-remote`, loaded by a thin `host-shell`) talking over a single `/api/*` REST surface to one **Java 21 / Spring Boot 3.3** service backed by **PostgreSQL 16.4**. The strategy side is a four-level **RCDO** tree (Rally Cry → Defining Objective → Outcome → Supporting Outcome); the work side is the **WeeklyCommit** aggregate of `CommitItem`s, each linked to a Supporting Outcome — and the link is enforced **at LOCK, not at save**. The whole system hangs off one representative action: an employee **locks their week**. That click travels Screen (07) → Frontend data layer (06, the single RTK Query slice) → Federation seam (05) → Security (03, JWT validated, member from the JWT subject) → Domain FSM (01, `LifecycleService.lock` guards, freezes an immutable snapshot, emits `commit.locked`) → API contract (02, returns a `CommitDto` or RFC-7807 `ProblemDetail`) → Integrations (04, the event drives an Outlook calendar write as a fault-isolated side effect). Reconciliation and the manager roll-up follow the same spine, with the **snapshot-vs-live-status** diff powering planned-vs-actual. Cross-cutting: one auth identity threads both halves, one RFC-7807 error shape runs end-to-end, every external secret is an empty-safe env ref, and Spring profiles flip integrations on and off.

layers.name: Domain & Lifecycle
layers.plain:
This is the heart of the system: the data that models company strategy and weekly work, plus the rulebook that says how a week moves from draft to locked to reconciled to carried-forward.

The strategy side is a four-level tree called RCDO (Rally Cry → Defining Objective → Outcome → Supporting Outcome). The work side is a WeeklyCommit — one person's plan for one week, made of items that each link to a Supporting Outcome.

A single component, the LifecycleService, owns every legal state change. It is deliberately a "pure" object that holds no database — it checks a move is allowed, changes the objects in memory, and hands them back for the caller to save. Because it has no database, the most important rules (a week can only lock when every item is linked, content is editable only in DRAFT, a lock freezes a permanent snapshot of the plan) can be tested exhaustively without one.
layers.deep:
The strategy side is four JPA entities, each child carrying a NOT-NULL parent FK enforced at the schema (V3): `RallyCry` (`backend/src/main/java/com/solovis/wcm/rcdo/RallyCry.java:24`) → `DefiningObjective` (`DefiningObjective.java:30`) → `Outcome` (`Outcome.java:30`) → `SupportingOutcome` (`SupportingOutcome.java:29`, the leaf a `CommitItem` links to). Window dates exist on every level but containment is intentionally **not** enforced.

The work side is the `WeeklyCommit` aggregate (`WeeklyCommit.java:32`) — `memberId`+`weekStart` UNIQUE, a `lifecycleState`, and a **transient** `items` list (not a JPA association, so children persist via their own repository and `ddl-auto: validate` stays honest). `CommitItem` (`CommitItem.java:24`) has a **nullable** `supportingOutcomeId` (KTD5), a `chessTier`, a live `status`, and a self-link `carriedFromItemId`.

The FSM is `LifecycleService` (`LifecycleService.java:19`), holding **no repositories**. Its surface: `lock(commit, lockedAt) → CommitSnapshot` (the guarded DRAFT→LOCKED move, `:39`), `startReconciling` (`:66`), `reconcile` (`:75`, forces the `ManagerReview` REVIEWED), `carryForward` (`:92`), and `assertItemEditAllowed(commit, contentChanged)` (`:126`). The five states are in `LifecycleState` (`LifecycleState.java:6`); legal edges are enumerated in `LifecycleTransition` (`LifecycleTransition.java:10`) so an un-enumerated edge is illegal by construction.

**Reconciliation = immutable snapshot vs live status (KTD4).** At LOCK, `SnapshotItem.freeze(...)` copies each item's **text/link/tier only — never status** (`SnapshotItem.java:74`) and captures the source `CommitItem.id` as the deterministic join key (`:37`). Thereafter `CommitItem.status` carries the mutable ACTUAL, editable only while RECONCILING. The diff joins on `commitItemId`; a live item with no snapshot row is flagged `ADDED_AFTER_LOCK` (`ReconciliationService.java:157`).

**Design decisions:** (1) the FSM is repository-free so guards are unit-testable without a DB; (2) the link is nullable at the column but required at LOCK, so a draft can hold an unlinked item yet "every locked commitment is linked" stays true; `allItemsLinked()` explicitly requires a non-empty list because `allMatch` over empty is vacuously true (`WeeklyCommit.java:104`).

**Auditing:** every entity extends `AbstractAuditingEntity` (`AbstractAuditingEntity.java:30`), which adds `createdBy`/`createdDate`/... and uses assigned-UUID PKs + `Persistable.isNew()` to force INSERT (not merge) so a parent and child save in correct FK order in one transaction. `JpaConfig` resolves the auditor from the JWT subject, else `"system"`.

**Drift (corrected):** migrations are **V1..V10**, not V1..V8 (`V9__rcdo_owner_ids.sql`, `V10__member_timezone_and_notifications.sql` exist); RCDO `owner_id` is now on **all four** levels, not just the leaf.

components.name: LifecycleService
components.what: The repository-free FSM: guards lock / startReconciling / reconcile / carryForward / assertItemEditAllowed

components.name: WeeklyCommit
components.what: Aggregate root; memberId+weekStart UNIQUE, lifecycleState, transient items working set

components.name: CommitItem
components.what: One plan line; nullable supportingOutcomeId, chessTier, live status, carriedFromItemId

components.name: CommitSnapshot
components.what: The frozen plan captured at LOCK; exactly one per commit (UNIQUE)

components.name: SupportingOutcome
components.what: RCDO leaf; NOT-NULL outcomeId; the link target of a CommitItem

components.name: LifecycleTransition
components.what: The enumerated legal FSM edges + isLegal / legalTargets adjacency

components.name: ChessTier
components.what: Ordered priority enum KING > QUEEN > ROOK > BISHOP > KNIGHT > PAWN (declaration order = rank)

components.name: AbstractAuditingEntity
components.what: @MappedSuperclass adding audit columns + Persistable (assigned-UUID INSERT vs merge)

layers.connects: It hands the entities, FSM, and application services up to the API & Contract layer (02), which wraps them with authorization, paging, and RFC-7807 errors over HTTP, and its commit.locked event is consumed by Integrations (04).

layers.name: API & Contract
layers.plain:
This is the module's HTTP front door: a set of deliberately thin REST controllers under /api/* that turn JSON requests into service calls and back, carrying almost no logic of their own.

Who you are is always worked out on the server from your login token, never from anything you put in the request — which is the main defense against people poking at other users' data. Whether a route is for managers or admins is checked once, centrally; whether you may touch a specific row is checked inside each service.

Every error comes back in one standard shape (RFC-7807 "problem" JSON), and the whole surface is published as a machine-readable OpenAPI contract the frontend builds against.
layers.deep:
Controllers are thin `@RestController`s holding a single service field and forwarding — `CommitController` (`backend/src/main/java/com/solovis/wcm/commit/CommitController.java:28`), `ReconciliationController` (`ReconciliationController.java:23`), `RollupController` (`RollupController.java:31`), `RcdoAdminController` (`RcdoAdminController.java:34`), `OutlookController`, `GraphConsentController`, `SettingsController`, and the `@Profile("e2e")`-only `E2eResetController` (`E2eResetController.java:37`).

**Authorization model (where each check lives):** `SecurityConfig` (`SecurityConfig.java:44`) defines `MANAGER_SCOPE = "SCOPE_reconcile:commits"` (`:47`) and `ADMIN_RCDO_SCOPE = "SCOPE_admin:rcdo"` (`:50`). The single `@Profile("!e2e")` chain permit-lists `/actuator/health`, the OpenAPI docs, and `GET /api/graph/callback`; gates the roll-up, review-queue, per-commit review, the reconcile transitions, and `POST /api/integration/outlook/schedule` behind manager scope (`:74`-`:86`); gates `POST/PUT/DELETE /api/admin/rcdo/**` behind admin scope (`:90`-`:95`); and requires a valid JWT for everything else (`:97`). **Endpoint scope ≠ data scope** — even a manager-scoped route is row-filtered to the caller's own reports inside the service (e.g. `ReconciliationService.requireManagerOf`).

**The one open app route:** `GET /api/graph/callback` is `permitAll` because Entra redirects the browser there with no bearer token; it is instead guarded by a short-lived HMAC-signed `state` minted by `/connect` and verified on callback to derive the member (`GraphConsentController.java:55`).

**Error contract:** `ApiExceptionHandler` (`@RestControllerAdvice`, `ApiExceptionHandler.java:39`) maps every exception to a `ProblemDetail` with a stable `type` URN (`urn:wcm:problem:<code>`) and a `code` the FE branches on — 401 unresolved member, 403 ownership, 404 not-found, 409 illegal FSM transition (with `from`/`to`), 422 empty/unlinked submit, 400 invalid consent state, 503 Graph-not-configured, and explicit handlers for Spring's built-in MVC exceptions so they don't escape as a misleading bare 403 via `/error`.

**Paging:** `PageResponse<T>` is a deliberately **flat** record `{content,totalElements,totalPages,number,size}` (`PageResponse.java:11`), not Spring's nested `PagedModel`, because the FE `Page<T>` contract and the MSW mocks read the flat shape. Roll-up/review-queue cap size at `MAX_PAGE_SIZE = 2000` and `RollupController` rejects out-of-range pagination at the boundary with `@Min(0)`/`@Max(2000)`.

**Design decisions:** thin controllers / fat services keep authz and state in one tested place; the acting member comes from the JWT, never the body (KTD6, closes IDOR/BOLA); coarse scope at the edge plus fine ownership in services is defense-in-depth.

**Drift (corrected):** the TECHNICAL.md REST table omits `RcdoAdminController`, `SettingsController`, and `POST .../outlook/schedule`, and omits the `SCOPE_admin:rcdo` gate — all are real in the code.

components.name: CommitController
components.what: Weekly-commit CRUD + submit (DRAFT → LOCKED)

components.name: ReconciliationController
components.what: Reconcile transitions, item-status patch, planned-vs-actual diff, carry-forward

components.name: RollupController
components.what: Team roll-up (paged, manager-scoped) + dashboard drill-through resolver

components.name: SecurityConfig
components.what: The single always-on (!e2e) filter chain + manager/admin scope gates

components.name: ApiExceptionHandler
components.what: @RestControllerAdvice mapping every exception to an RFC-7807 ProblemDetail

components.name: PageResponse<T>
components.what: Flat page envelope {content,totalElements,totalPages,number,size} the FE reads directly

components.name: RcdoAdminController
components.what: Admin RCDO edit-tree CRUD at all 4 levels, gated by SCOPE_admin:rcdo

components.name: OpenApiConfig
components.what: springdoc OpenAPI bean (title, version 0.0.1) frozen into docs/openapi.yaml

layers.connects: It exposes the Domain & Lifecycle (01) core over HTTP, sits behind the Security & Authorization (03) gates, and is consumed by the Frontend Data Layer (06) RTK Query slice and the frozen docs/openapi.yaml.

layers.name: Security & Authorization
layers.plain:
The backend is an Auth0 OAuth2 resource server that defends every /api/* route with three stacked layers. First, you need a valid login token or you get a 401. Second, manager-only and admin-only routes need a specific permission or you get a 403. Third, even with the right permission, you can only act on your own rows — the system always works out who you are from your token, never from an id you send, which stops people reaching other users' data.

There is one exception route, the Microsoft consent callback, which arrives in the browser with no token; it is protected instead by a signed, short-lived ticket. For automated browser tests there is a separate, test-only login path that never ships to production, and Microsoft tokens are encrypted before they are stored.
layers.deep:
Three defense-in-depth layers protect `/api/*`: (1) **authentication** — a valid RS256 Auth0 JWT everywhere except a tiny allow-list (`SecurityConfig.java:55`); (2) **coarse authz** — manager routes need `SCOPE_reconcile:commits`, RCDO edit-tree mutations need `SCOPE_admin:rcdo` (`SecurityConfig.java:74`, `:90`); (3) **fine authz** — the acting member is always resolved from the JWT subject via `CurrentMemberProvider` (`CurrentMemberProvider.java:9`), never a client-supplied id, which closes IDOR/BOLA.

A single `SecurityFilterChain` is always active under `@Profile("!e2e")`. `jwtAuthenticationConverter` (`SecurityConfig.java:122`) maps **both** the space-delimited `scope` claim **and** Auth0's `permissions` array to `SCOPE_*` authorities, so route gates work whether the tenant uses scopes or RBAC permissions. The prod `auth0JwtDecoder` (`SecurityConfig.java:147`) is `@Conditional` on a non-blank issuer (`Auth0IssuerConfiguredCondition.java:12`) — so a bare/test boot still starts — and validates signature against the Auth0 JWKS plus issuer/expiry and, when set, audience via `AudienceValidator` (`AudienceValidator.java:14`).

**The tokenless Graph callback:** `GET /api/graph/callback` is `permitAll` because Entra redirects the browser there with no bearer. `/connect` mints a `state = base64url(memberId|expiry).base64url(HMAC-SHA256(payload))` via `GraphConsentState.issue(...)` (`GraphConsentState.java:55`); the callback's `verify(state)` checks the MAC in constant time (`MessageDigest.isEqual`) and enforces a 10-minute TTL, deriving the member **from the verified state, not from any principal** — a forged/stale state throws `InvalidConsentStateException` → 400 before any token is bound.

**Hermetic E2E (KTD13):** under `@Profile("e2e")`, `E2eSecurityConfig` (`E2eSecurityConfig.java:47`) replaces the JWT chain with a `DebugMemberFilter` that resolves an `X-Debug-Member` header to a seeded member; seeded MANAGERs get `SCOPE_reconcile:commits` and the top-level exec also gets `SCOPE_admin:rcdo`, so the **same** route gates apply as in prod. It is strictly test-path — the prod impls are `@Profile("!e2e")`, so the header path can never be reached in prod.

**At rest / secrets:** Graph tokens are AES-256-GCM encrypted by `TokenCipher` (`TokenCipher.java:19`), which reuses the same 256-bit `wcm.graph.token-enc-key` that `GraphConsentState` signs with. All external secrets (`AUTH0_ISSUER_URI`, `AUTH0_AUDIENCE`, `GRAPH_TOKEN_ENC_KEY`, the SNS/SQS refs) default empty, so the app boots unconfigured and `TokenCipher`/`GraphConsentState` fail only at invocation, not startup.

**Drift (corrected):** Graph consent is an **authorization-code exchange with an optional client_secret**, not the PKCE flow some older docs describe — there is no `code_verifier`/`code_challenge` in the code.

components.name: SecurityConfig
components.what: Prod/default/test filter chain (@Profile("!e2e")): permit-list, scope gates, prod JWT decoder

components.name: jwtAuthenticationConverter
components.what: Maps the scope claim AND Auth0 permissions array to SCOPE_* authorities

components.name: CurrentMemberProvider
components.what: The acting-member port (KTD6): resolve who is acting, always from the JWT, never a body

components.name: JwtCurrentMemberProvider
components.what: Prod impl: member from JWT sub, JIT-provisioned via MemberProvisioningService

components.name: GraphConsentState
components.what: HMAC-SHA256 signer/verifier of the consent state (CSRF / code-injection guard, 10-min TTL)

components.name: TokenCipher
components.what: AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt of Graph tokens at rest (base64(IV ‖ ciphertext+tag))

components.name: E2eSecurityConfig
components.what: @Profile("e2e") chain: X-Debug-Member authenticator over seeded members; never prod

components.name: ProblemAuthHandlers
components.what: RFC-7807 problem+json writers for filter-chain 401/403 so denials match the service error shape

layers.connects: It guards the routes the API & Contract layer (02) exposes and supplies the trusted acting-member seam those services use, and it provides the signed consent state and token encryption the Integrations layer (04) relies on for Graph.

layers.name: Integrations & Eventing
layers.plain:
This layer handles the two things WCM reaches out to the outside world for: writing events to Microsoft Outlook calendars, and passing internal events between parts of the system. Both sit behind "ports" — swappable seams — so the app boots and runs its tests with no live Microsoft or AWS credentials.

When someone locks a week, an internal commit.locked event is raised; a consumer picks it up and asks the calendar port to create an Outlook event. Crucially, if that calendar write fails, it does NOT undo the lock — the calendar is a nice-to-have side effect, not part of the core transaction. By default events are dispatched in-process; flip the AWS profile and the same events flow through SNS → SQS instead, with consumers de-duplicating so a redelivered event never double-books.
layers.deep:
Two external integrations sit behind ports. **(A) Outlook / Microsoft Graph:** `CalendarSyncPort` (`CalendarSyncPort.java:9`) has **two** methods — `syncLockedCommit(LockedCommitSync)` (idempotent by `commitId`) and `scheduleEvent(ScheduledEventCommand)` (the CB-1 manager 1:1). `StubCalendarAdapter` (`@Profile("!graph")`, `StubCalendarAdapter.java:18`) records calls in memory; `GraphCalendarAdapter` (`@Profile("graph")`, `GraphCalendarAdapter.java:31`) POSTs a real delegated event to `/me/events` and sets `transactionId = commitId` so Graph itself dedups redeliveries.

**Token lifecycle:** each member's `GraphToken` (`GraphToken.java:30`, `UNIQUE(member_id)`) stores AES-GCM **ciphertext** only — plaintext is never persisted. `GraphTokenService` (`GraphTokenService.java:23`) does `exchangeCode(memberId, code)` (POST the authorization-code grant, upsert one encrypted row), `validAccessToken(memberId)` (refresh within a 5-minute skew of expiry, only if a refresh token exists), and `isConnected(memberId)`.

**Lock → event → consumer → calendar:** the LOCK transaction commits, then `EventPublisher.publish(DomainEvent.of("commit.locked", ...))` fires. The default `InProcessEventPublisher` (`@Profile("!aws")`, `InProcessEventPublisher.java:16`) re-publishes on the Spring context; `CommitLockedCalendarConsumer` (`CommitLockedCalendarConsumer.java:29`) handles it via `syncOnce` — dedup on `eventId`, check the `createEventOnLock` preference, call `CalendarSyncPort.syncLockedCommit`, then `recordLockSync`. A Graph error inside this step is caught and logged **non-fatally** — the lock already committed, so calendar sync never rolls it back (`CommitLockedCalendarConsumer.java:58`-`:60`).

**AWS path (SNS → SQS):** under `@Profile("aws")`, `SnsEventPublisher` (`SnsEventPublisher.java:22`) publishes JSON to an SNS topic with a `type` message attribute; SNS fans out to SQS; `SqsEventPoller` (`SqsEventPoller.java:27`) long-polls (wait 20s, batch 10), and `EventDispatcher.dispatch` routes `commit.locked` to `handleForRedelivery` whose failure **propagates** so SQS redelivers and eventually redrives to the DLQ. The two failure modes (in-process swallows, SQS re-throws) share one idempotent `syncOnce` core. All AWS beans are profile-gated, so the default boot needs no AWS creds.

**Design decisions:** port + profile-gated adapters keep the domain free of Graph/AWS; sync is a post-commit side effect, not in the transaction; idempotency exists at three layers (consumer `eventId` dedup, adapter `transactionId`/`computeIfAbsent`, SQS redrive). The CB-1 schedule is deliberately **not** `@Transactional` because a DB transaction should not span the outbound Graph HTTP call.

**Gotchas:** `scheduleEvent` is **not** idempotent (no `transactionId`), so a retried schedule can double-book; the in-process listener returns empty on a swallowed error so callers can't tell "skipped" from "failed-and-swallowed"; `handledEventIds` dedup state is per-process and in-memory.

**Drift (corrected):** `CalendarSyncPort` has **two** methods (not one); the consent flow is authorization-code with an optional client_secret, not PKCE.

components.name: CalendarSyncPort
components.what: Outbound calendar port with two methods: syncLockedCommit (idempotent) + scheduleEvent (CB-1)

components.name: GraphCalendarAdapter
components.what: @Profile("graph") real delegated POST /me/events; transactionId = commitId for dedup

components.name: CommitLockedCalendarConsumer
components.what: commit.locked → calendar sync; idempotent on eventId; swallows (in-process) vs re-throws (SQS)

components.name: GraphTokenService
components.what: Authorization-code exchange, encrypted token store, refresh-before-use within a 5-min skew

components.name: EventPublisher
components.what: Outbound domain-event port; contract: must not throw to the caller's path

components.name: SnsEventPublisher
components.what: @Profile("aws") publish event JSON to SNS with a type message attribute for filtering

components.name: SqsEventPoller
components.what: @Profile("aws") long-poll the queue, dispatch each message, ack cleanly-handled / leave failures for redrive

components.name: OutlookService
components.what: Connection read model, prefs (createEventOnLock defaults true), system-side sync record, CB-1 schedule

layers.connects: It is triggered by the Domain & Lifecycle (01) commit.locked event and relies on Security (03) for the signed consent state and token encryption; its Outlook/Graph REST routes are exposed through the API & Contract layer (02).

layers.name: Micro-Frontend Shell & Federation
layers.plain:
The frontend is split into two separate apps that join up at runtime using Module Federation: a thin host-shell (the consumer) and the wc-remote (the producer that actually contains the Weekly Commit feature). The host fetches the remote live over the network rather than bundling it, proving the two really are independent.

The remote is built to run both ways — on its own during development and tests, or embedded inside the host. When embedded, the host owns the page's address bar and router, and shares one copy of React and the router with the remote so they don't collide. Login also crosses this seam: a small AuthBridge picks where the access token comes from and hands it to the data layer.
layers.deep:
Two Vite apps joined by Module Federation: `host-shell` (consumer, port 4200) and `wc-remote` (producer, port 4201). The host declares one remote `wc` and lazy-loads two surfaces it exposes — the full `wc/WeeklyCommitApp` and a self-contained `wc/WeeklyCommitWidget` — fetching them live over MF with **no SPA fallback** (`apps/host-shell/src/App.tsx:22-33`). The remote names itself `wc`, sets `filename: remoteEntry.js`, and lists the two `exposes` (`apps/wc-remote/vite.config.ts:49-64`).

**Shared-singleton contract:** both configs declare `react`, `react-dom` (`^18.3.1`) and `react-router-dom` (`^6.26.1`) as `singleton: true` (`apps/host-shell/vite.config.ts:32-38`). The router **must** be a singleton because the host owns the `BrowserRouter` and the remote consumes `useNavigate`/`useLocation` from that one context — two copies would break it.

**Boot order:** `main.tsx` does `import('./bootstrap')` — an async boundary so the MF runtime + singletons finish initializing before any module that statically imports React runs (`apps/host-shell/src/main.tsx:5-9`). `bootstrap.tsx` mounts `<App/>` into `#root`.

**Routing decision:** `App` checks `window.location.pathname`. `/dashboard` renders `DashboardDemo` (host chrome around the federated widget); every other URL renders `ModuleView`, which wraps the full remote in a host `BrowserRouter` so the real browser URL drives the remote's internal routes — both inside a `RemoteErrorBoundary` (`apps/host-shell/src/App.tsx:36-67`) that shows a message instead of a white screen on a failed `remoteEntry` load.

**Standalone-but-remote-ready remote:** `WeeklyCommitApp` (`apps/wc-remote/src/WeeklyCommitApp.tsx:111-127`) takes all-optional props — `getToken?`, `user?`, and `router?` — defaulting to a `MemoryRouter` + self-Auth0, so the identical component boots in `vite dev`, Vitest, and embedded. In practice the host passes only `router` today and leaves auth to the remote. Inside, it mounts `AppProviders` (Redux → Auth0 → `AuthBridge`) → `RequireAuth` → router → `WcShell`. `AuthBridge` (`AuthBridge.tsx:51-135`) picks one token source by deterministic priority and registers it with `@wcm/api`. The widget owns an **isolated per-instance Redux store** (`useRef(makeStore())`), never the app-shell singleton, so a host can drop multiple tiles without collision.

**Cross-origin:** `corsForRemoteEntry` attaches `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` on both `vite` and `vite preview` so the host (:4200) can import the remote's `remoteEntry.js` (:4201); both apps build with `target: esnext`, `minify: false`, and `allowedHosts: true` for Dockerized E2E.

**No hardcoded host chrome:** screens never call the router directly — thin route adapters in `routes.tsx` are the only place `useNavigate` is called, satisfying the "no hardcoded shell/navigation" NFR.

**Gotchas:** there is no MF runtime under Vitest (tests resolve `wc/*` to placeholder stubs); MF expose names must match exactly on both sides; widget navigation via `onOpen` resolves to `window.location.assign` (a hard navigation, not a router push); `VITE_REMOTE_ENTRY` must repoint the remote to `host.docker.internal:4201` for Dockerized E2E.

components.name: Host federation config
components.what: Declares remote wc (type: module, env-overridable entry) + shared react/react-dom/react-router-dom singletons

components.name: Host App shell
components.what: Header chrome, RemoteErrorBoundary, /dashboard demo vs full-module switch

components.name: Host async boundary
components.what: main.tsx import('./bootstrap') so the MF runtime + singletons init before static React imports

components.name: Remote federation config
components.what: name: wc, filename: remoteEntry.js, two exposes, shared singletons, esnext/minify:false

components.name: WeeklyCommitApp
components.what: Federated entry: optional host props; providers → RequireAuth → router (MemoryRouter default) → WcShell

components.name: AuthBridge
components.what: Host↔remote auth seam: picks one token source by priority and registers the getter with @wcm/api

components.name: WeeklyCommitWidget
components.what: Second expose: self-contained dashboard tile with its own per-instance store; onOpen callback to host

components.name: corsForRemoteEntry
components.what: Attaches ACAO header on dev serve + preview for the cross-origin remoteEntry.js import

layers.connects: It mounts the Screens & Design System (07) under the host's router and registers (via AuthBridge) the token getter that the Frontend Data Layer (06) uses; the host's dev proxy points /api at the Spring backend.

layers.name: Frontend Data Layer
layers.plain:
This is the single road the frontend uses to talk to the backend — no screen ever calls fetch or axios directly. It is one RTK Query slice that knows every endpoint, attaches your login token to every request, and caches what comes back.

The clever part is cache coherence: each piece of data is tagged, and each change knows which tags to bust, so after you submit a week the week list and that commit re-fetch themselves automatically with no manual wiring. It also ships a complete in-memory fake backend (MSW) that mirrors the real contract one-to-one, so the whole frontend can build, run tests, and even drive end-to-end browser tests with no live server running.
layers.deep:
`libs/api` is the **only** path app code uses to reach the backend (`libs/api/src/commitApi.ts:1-2`). It is one RTK Query slice, `commitApi` (`commitApi.ts:100-446`), whose `baseQuery.prepareHeaders` attaches auth per request: `X-Debug-Member` in the hermetic E2E path, else `Authorization: Bearer <token>` from an injectable provider, else **no header** (empty-safe). The base URL defaults to `/api` (same-origin, then host-proxied to Spring) and is overridable with `VITE_API_BASE`; `resolveApiBase` absolutizes a relative base for undici under jsdom.

**Endpoint groups (all under `/api`):** commits (incl. `getCurrentWeek` transforming a 204 to `null`), reconciliation + carry-forward, RCDO browse + admin CRUD, review/roll-up/review-queue, pulse, Outlook integration, and settings (`commitApi.ts:104-446`).

**Cache coherence** is a `providesTags`/`invalidatesTags` graph over **eleven** `tagTypes` (`libs/api/src/tags.ts:5-17`): `Commit`, `WeekList`, `Reconciliation`, `RcdoTree`, `SupportingOutcome`, `Review`, `Rollup`, `ReviewQueue`, `Pulse`, `Outlook`, `Settings`. Notable edges — `submitCommit` busts `commitTag(id)` + `weekListTag()` (`commitApi.ts:145-149`); `reviewCommit` fans out to **four** tags (review, commit, roll-up, review-queue) because a review changes all of them; any RCDO admin mutation busts the single `rcdoTreeTag()` so the picker/browse refetch; `scheduleOutlookEvent` invalidates **nothing** (the event lives in Outlook, not a WCM read model).

**The store** is built by `makeStore()` (`libs/api/src/store.ts:9-18`) — `commitApi` reducer + middleware + `setupListeners` (refetch-on-focus/reconnect) — and each call returns a **fresh isolated store** so tests and each embedded widget don't collide.

**The token seam** `tokenProvider.ts` exposes `setTokenGetter`/`getAccessToken`/`setDebugMember`/`clearTokenGetter`; `baseQuery` reads them in order — debug member **wins** (sends `X-Debug-Member`, no Bearer), else a token → Bearer, else nothing — so the two auth headers are mutually exclusive.

**The MSW mock backend** (`libs/api/src/msw/handlers.ts`) is a full in-memory REST backend mirroring the contract 1:1: a `commits`/`pulses`/`outlook`/`account`/`notifications` store and a mutable `rcdoTree`, `resetMockDb()` between tests, RFC-7807 `problem()` errors, and the **same lifecycle guards** as the server (PUT only while DRAFT → 409, submit requires every item linked → 422, status patch only while RECONCILING → 409). Even the mock is server-owned identity: `createCommit` stamps `MOCK_MEMBER_ID` and ignores a client-supplied `memberId`.

**The wire types** are hand-written DTOs in `libs/types/src/contract.ts`, mirrored 1:1 from the Java records — **not** generated; conformance is proven by the backend's MockMvc tests, so DTO drift is invisible until a contract test fails.

**Gotchas:** MSW route order matters (`/commits/current` and literal `/commits` are declared before `/commits/:id`); the mock DB is module-global mutable state needing `resetMockDb()` between cases; `Page<T>` is a subset of Spring's page envelope.

components.name: commitApi slice
components.what: The RTK Query createApi with every endpoint, generated hooks, and the tag graph

components.name: baseQuery
components.what: fetchBaseQuery + prepareHeaders attaching X-Debug-Member or Authorization: Bearer (empty-safe)

components.name: Tag vocabulary
components.what: The 11 tagTypes + typed id helpers (commitTag, weekListTag, reviewQueueTag, settingsTag, ...)

components.name: makeStore
components.what: Fresh isolated store: commitApi reducer + middleware + setupListeners (focus/reconnect refetch)

components.name: Token provider
components.what: Injectable getter + debug-member seam: setTokenGetter / getAccessToken / setDebugMember

components.name: MSW handlers
components.what: Full in-memory mock REST backend mirroring the contract 1:1, with lifecycle guards + RFC-7807 errors

components.name: resetMockDb
components.what: Resets the in-memory DB + RCDO tree between tests so state never leaks across cases

components.name: Contract DTOs/enums
components.what: Hand-written types mirroring the Java records 1:1 (not generated); conformance proven by MockMvc tests

layers.connects: AuthBridge in the Federation layer (05) registers the token getter here; this slice issues requests to the API & Contract surface (02) and is consumed exclusively by the Screens & Design System (07) hooks.

layers.name: Screens & Design System
layers.plain:
This is everything a person actually sees: the screens of the Weekly Commit feature and the visual design system that paints them. The screens map one-to-one onto the weekly cycle — write a draft, see the current week, lock it, reconcile what happened, browse history, and (for managers) review reports, roll up the team, and schedule a 1:1.

Each screen is thin: it drives its whole UI off a single data query's loading / empty / error / data states and receives navigation as plain callback props, so it never knows about URLs or routers. The design system defines every color once, in one place, and feeds it into the stylesheet, the Tailwind utilities, and typed runtime maps. Accessibility is built in: status is never shown by color alone, dialogs trap focus, and lists are keyboard-operable.
layers.deep:
**Screens** live in `apps/wc-remote/src/screens` and are RTK-Query-driven views taking navigation as **callback props** (no router imports). Employee screens: `MyWeeklyCommit` (`MyWeeklyCommit.tsx:84`, the current-week home that switches on `lifecycleState` and gates Lock on all-linked), `EditCommit` (`EditCommit.tsx:200`, the composer), `Reconciliation` (`Reconciliation.tsx:59`), `CommitHistory`, `PastCommitDetail`, `RcdoBrowser`, `Settings`. Manager screens: `ReviewQueue`, `ReviewDetail`, `RollupDashboard`, `ScheduleDialog`, and the federated `WeeklyCommitWidget`.

**Key verified behaviors:** `EditCommit` hydrates a local editing copy once, renders a `@dnd-kit` sortable list with a **keyboard-accessible** grip, autosaves **debounced 400 ms** via `useUpdateCommitMutation`, and on **Submit & lock** (`useSubmitCommitMutation`, `disabled={!canSubmit}` where `canSubmit = items.length > 0 && unlinked === 0 && blank === 0`) **flushes the pending autosave first** (`EditCommit.tsx:380`) so the lock never freezes a stale plan and a late PUT can't 409 a LOCKED commit. `Reconciliation` splits rows into planned vs `added-after-lock`, renders a two-column PLANNED-vs-ACTUAL grid, and debounces per-item status edits **300 ms** with local-optimistic state. `ScheduleDialog` sends `startDateTime` as an **ISO string carrying the local UTC offset** (`toIsoWithOffset`) so DST resolves correctly, and maps a `409 illegal_state` to an inline "Connect Outlook in Settings" error. `WeeklyCommitWidget` **owns its own RTK store** (`makeStore()` per mount).

**Design system (`libs/ui`):** the palette is authored **once** in `libs/ui/src/theme.colors.mjs` as plain JS (Tailwind can't import the `.ts` tokens), then mirrored three ways — into `global.css` `:root` CSS custom properties, into `tailwind.config.js` color utilities, and into typed runtime maps in `tokens.ts` (`LIFECYCLE_VISUAL`, `CHESS_*`, `RCDO_LEVEL`). Components style themselves with CSS-var inline styles + a small set of `.btn-*`/`.kicker`/`.tnum`/`.panel` utility classes + Tailwind utilities — **no** CSS modules, **no** styled-components. Type is IBM Plex Sans/Mono/Serif. There are **5** lifecycle states (each a text+icon badge: DRAFT/slate, LOCKED/cyan, RECONCILING/amber, RECONCILED/green, CARRY_FORWARD/violet) and **6** chess tiers (KING→PAWN), with chess emphasis restrained to line/tone, not hue.

**Accessibility is structural:** status is never color-alone (`LifecycleBadge` carries text + icon), dialogs trap focus (`Scrim`), reorder is keyboard-operable.

**Drift (corrected):** the barrel and screens claim "no Flowbite," but `flowbite-react` is **still a dependency**, still wired into `tailwind.config.js`, and still imported by one component (`WeekSelector.tsx`) — so it is **wired-but-being-phased-out**, not removed; the manager screens deliberately bypass it with a native `<select>`. And the "jest-axe tested" claim is not literal — a `jest-axe` type shim exists but **no test invokes `axe()`**; the a11y invariants are asserted directly in RTL specs (text + `<svg>` + `data-state`). Also: the `RcdoBrowser` Edit-tree affordance is gated best-effort on `account.canReview` because no `canAdmin` flag exists on the contract yet — a UX gate only; real authz is server-side.

components.name: MyWeeklyCommit
components.what: Current-week home; switches on lifecycleState across DRAFT/LOCKED/RECONCILING/RECONCILED + empty/loading/error; Lock gated on all-linked

components.name: EditCommit
components.what: The composer: dnd-kit drag-reorder, chess tier, RCDO link, 400 ms debounced autosave, Submit & lock (flush-then-submit)

components.name: Reconciliation
components.what: Planned-vs-actual two-column compare; per-item status 300 ms debounced PATCH; carry-forward + mark-reconciled

components.name: RollupDashboard
components.what: 4 metric tiles + sparkline + sortable paginated reports table with drill-through (lazy report query)

components.name: theme.colors.mjs
components.what: The single-source OKLCH palette + radii/shadows/fonts, mirrored to CSS vars, Tailwind, and tokens.ts

components.name: tokens.ts
components.what: Typed re-export + LIFECYCLE_VISUAL / CHESS_* / RCDO_LEVEL / CSS_VARS runtime maps

components.name: LifecycleBadge
components.what: The one lifecycle pill (text + icon, non-color-only) proving the a11y invariant via RTL

components.name: RcdoPickerDrawer
components.what: Right-drawer 4-level RCDO picker (search + keyboard drill-in); emits an RcdoSelection on leaf-select

layers.connects: Its screens read and write exclusively through the Frontend Data Layer (06) hooks, and the route table that mounts them is supplied by the Federation layer (05) host shell, which also embeds the WeeklyCommitWidget.

layers.name: Testing & Quality Gates
layers.plain:
The whole module is verified by five layers of tests wired into one CI pipeline, each with a hard gate that fails the build if it is breached. Backend unit tests prove the core rules with no database; backend integration tests run the real app against a real Postgres in a container; frontend tests run the UI against the mock backend; end-to-end tests drive the actual federated app through a real browser; and performance tests check the speed and pagination requirements hold.

On top of the tests, the build also enforces formatting, static analysis, and code-coverage thresholds, so a change that drops below the bar can't merge.
layers.deep:
A **five-layer test strategy** is wired into `.github/workflows/ci.yml`, each with a hard gate:

1. **Backend unit** — pure JUnit 5, the FSM exemplar being `LifecycleServiceTest` (`backend/src/test/java/com/solovis/wcm/commit/LifecycleServiceTest.java:22`): the full legal/illegal edge matrix, lock guard, snapshot immutability, carry-forward — no Spring/DB.
2. **Backend integration** — `*IT.java` extend `AbstractWebIT` (`AbstractWebIT.java:32`): `@SpringBootTest` + `@AutoConfigureMockMvc` + `@ActiveProfiles("test")` against a **process-wide singleton Testcontainers Postgres 16.4** (`WcmPostgresContainer.java:12`, started once, never stopped, `max_connections=300`), authenticating with a **local RS256 keypair** (`TestJwtConfig.java:34`), each test rolled back via `@Transactional`. Notable ITs: `CommitControllerIT` (spoofed-memberId ignored, cross-member read 403), `CommitLockedConsumerIT`, `SnsSqsEventingIT` (`@ActiveProfiles({"test","aws"})` on a **LocalStack** container), `GraphCalendarAdapterTest` (real adapter vs OkHttp MockWebServer), `SecurityIntegrationTest` (forged test-JWT rejected by the prod decoder).
3. **Frontend unit/component** — Vitest workspace (jsdom + `@vitejs/plugin-react`) with MSW-mocked API.
4. **E2E** — Cypress + cucumber Gherkin (3 `.feature` files / 6 scenarios) driving the **live federated app** over the real backend, plus one Playwright smoke; each scenario starts with `cy.resetData()` against the `@Profile("e2e")` reset endpoint.
5. **Performance** — k6 scenarios (`perf/plan-retrieval.js`, `rollup-pagination.js`, `lifecycle-throughput.js`) against a `@Profile("stress")` ~2000-record dataset, with **HARD p95<200ms / zero-error / pagination-invariant thresholds** (k6 exits 99 on breach).

**Two auth paths, two profiles:** ITs use the `test`-profile JWT; perf + browser E2E use the `e2e`-profile `X-Debug-Member` — they are not interchangeable.

**Quality gates** bind to `mvn verify` — Spotless, SpotBugs, and JaCoCo (merged ut+it, BUNDLE line **≥0.80**, with honest excludes for boot/config/glue and the two `@Profile("e2e")` classes) — and to the FE pipeline (TypeScript strict `tsc --noEmit`, ESLint 9 flat, Prettier 3.3.3).

**Drift (corrected):** the frontend coverage gate is **80%** on all four metrics (`vitest.config.ts:33-38`), **not** the 70% TECHNICAL.md claims, and it lives in `vitest.config.ts`, **not** `vitest.workspace.ts` (the workspace file defers coverage to root). The testing-matrix counts in TECHNICAL.md are also stale: the tree now has ~44 FE test files, 36 BE test files with 201 `@Test` annotations, and three k6 scenarios.

**Gotchas:** the `<200ms` IT assert is wall-clock and machine-dependent (the authoritative latency gate is the k6 p95); count-sensitive seed tests (`DemoSeederIT`) assume every other web IT rolled back; the `live` CI job runs zero tests today (`-Dgroups=live` matches no `@Tag("live")` suite yet).

components.name: AbstractWebIT
components.what: @SpringBootTest + MockMvc base; @ActiveProfiles("test"), @Import(TestJwtConfig), @Transactional rollback

components.name: WcmPostgresContainer
components.what: Process-wide singleton postgres:16.4, started once and never stopped; max_connections=300

components.name: LifecycleServiceTest
components.what: Pure FSM unit test (no Spring/DB): full legal/illegal edge matrix, lock guard, snapshot immutability

components.name: TestJwtConfig
components.what: @Profile("test") local RS256 keypair: mint / bearer / employee / manager post-processors

components.name: SnsSqsEventingIT
components.what: @ActiveProfiles({"test","aws"}) on a LocalStack container: publish → SNS → SQS → poll → consume once

components.name: Vitest coverage gate
components.what: v8 provider, all:true, 80% lines/functions/branches/statements (the corrected number)

components.name: Backend Maven gates
components.what: Spotless, SpotBugs, JaCoCo merge+check ≥0.80, Failsafe ITs bound to mvn verify

components.name: CI pipeline
components.what: be / fe / e2e / stress jobs + a gated (currently empty) live job

layers.connects: It is cross-cutting: it proves the FSM and HTTP contract from layers 01-02, the auth and eventing seams from 03-04, and the host/remote MF boundary from 05-07, all under the Spring profile matrix that Build & Deploy (09) defines.

layers.name: Build, Monorepo & Deployment
layers.plain:
This layer is how the whole thing is assembled and shipped. It is a two-toolchain monorepo: the frontend uses npm workspaces plus Nx and is built with Vite 5 and Module Federation; the backend is a Maven / Spring Boot / Java 21 service. Locally, a single Docker Compose file runs Postgres.

What the app actually does in each environment is chosen entirely by Spring profiles — flags that flip a specific seam (login, seed data, calendar adapter, event publisher) on or off with no code change. The AWS cloud deployment is a documented design and runbook rather than a standing live stack: the SNS→SQS event seam is built and tested against a local AWS emulator, but the cloud servers themselves are gated on credentials and cost approval.
layers.deep:
A **two-toolchain monorepo**: an **npm-workspaces + Nx** frontend (`apps/*` + `libs/*`, `package.json:7-10`) built with **Vite 5 + Module Federation**, and a **Maven / Spring Boot 3.3.13 / Java 21** backend (`backend/pom.xml`). Local infra is one `docker-compose.yml` Postgres 16.4.

**Path aliases / strict TS:** `tsconfig.base.json:26` maps `@wcm/types`/`@wcm/api`/`@wcm/ui` to `libs/*/src`; strict options include `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `noImplicitReturns`, `noFallthroughCasesInSwitch`. **Version pins:** `@module-federation/vite` is pinned **exactly** to `1.7.0` (not `^`, because the MF/Vite integration is version-sensitive), Vite `^5.4.2`, Vitest `^2.1.9`, Nx `^19.6.0`; engines require Node ≥20 / npm ≥10.

**Profile/env contract:** every external secret in `application.yml` is an env ref defaulting to **empty**, so the app boots with nothing configured; a non-blank value (or a profile) activates the integration. The event seam flips in-process ↔ SNS/SQS purely via the `aws` profile + `WCM_SNS_TOPIC_ARN`/`WCM_SQS_QUEUE_URL`. **Schema contract:** Hibernate runs `ddl-auto: validate` against the Flyway-migrated schema, refusing to start if entities and migrations diverge.

**Spring profiles matrix:** `default` (in-process publisher / stub calendar / prod JWT / no seed), `test` (imports `TestJwtConfig`), `e2e` (replaces the JWT chain with `E2eSecurityConfig` + `X-Debug-Member`, enables the reset endpoint), `demo` (`DemoSeeder` loads the SOLOVIS_SEED RCDO tree + member graph), `stress` (`StressSeeder` bulk-loads ~210 reports / ~2100 commits / ~8400 items), `graph` (swaps the stub for the real `GraphCalendarAdapter`), `aws` (`SnsEventPublisher` + `SqsEventPoller` + AWS clients). Profiles compose: ITs run `test`; perf runs `e2e,demo,stress`; browser E2E runs `e2e,demo`; the SNS→SQS IT runs `test,aws`.

**Deploy (design + runbook only):** backend → ECR/EKS with `/actuator/health` readiness/liveness, RDS Postgres 16.4, the federated FE (`host-shell` + `wc-remote`'s `remoteEntry.js`) → S3 + CloudFront, and an SNS + SQS + DLQ event seam that flips on by activating `aws` and setting the two ARNs. **EKS/S3/CloudFront provisioning is NOT executed by default CI** — it is gated on AWS creds + explicit cost approval; the SNS→SQS seam itself is built and LocalStack-tested.

**Design decisions:** profile-driven seams (not config branches) so flipping environments is a profile change with no code edit; a single OKLCH token source drives Tailwind + `global.css` + typed tokens; `react-router-dom` MUST be an MF singleton or the host's `BrowserRouter` context breaks the remote.

**Drift (corrected):** the brief lists **Yarn** workspaces; this build deliberately uses **npm** workspaces (same `apps/*`+`libs/*` shape, Nx kept for the task graph; PRD line 77 says PA's package manager need not be replicated). Migrations are **V1..V10**, not V1..V8. `docker-compose.yml` maps host port **5433** while `.env.example`/`application.yml` default to `:5432`, so a bare local run against the compose DB must override the port (the CI/e2e/stress harnesses set `:5433` explicitly).

components.name: Workspaces + scripts
components.what: apps/* + libs/* npm workspaces; scripts build / test / lint / format / typecheck

components.name: Nx task graph
components.what: targetDefaults for build/lint/test with caching; production named-input excludes test/config

components.name: TS path aliases
components.what: @wcm/types → libs/types/src, @wcm/api → libs/api/src, @wcm/ui → libs/ui/src

components.name: Host Vite config
components.what: Declares the wc remote (VITE_REMOTE_ENTRY override); shares react/react-dom/react-router-dom as singletons

components.name: Maven build
components.what: Spring Boot 3.3.13, Java 21, Flyway, springdoc, oauth2-resource-server, AWS SDK v2 BOM

components.name: Flyway migrations
components.what: V1..V10 applied at boot (the corrected ceiling; not V1..V8)

components.name: Spring core config
components.what: Datasource, Hibernate validate + UTC + no-OIV, Auth0/Graph/AWS env refs (empty-safe), actuator health-only

components.name: Local infra
components.what: postgres:16.4, host 5433 → container 5432, healthcheck, named volume wcm-pgdata

layers.connects: It is cross-cutting: it produces the host/remote MF bundle for the Federation layer (05) and the Spring jar for the backend, and defines the profile matrix and CI jobs that the Testing & Quality layer (08) runs every test under.

flow.title: Press Submit & Lock
flow.plain: In the composer, the employee has added items, linked each to a piece of strategy, and set a priority. They press the Submit & Lock button — which only becomes clickable once there is at least one item and every item is linked.
flow.deep: In `EditCommit`, **Submit & lock** uses `useSubmitCommitMutation` and is `disabled={!canSubmit}` where `canSubmit = items.length > 0 && unlinked === 0 && blank === 0` (`apps/wc-remote/src/screens/EditCommit.tsx:335`). `doLock` **flushes the pending 400 ms autosave first** (`EditCommit.tsx:380`) so the lock never freezes a stale plan and a late PUT can't 409 against a now-LOCKED commit. This UX pre-check mirrors the server guard — the server re-checks.

flow.title: useSubmitCommitMutation → POST /api/commits/{id}/submit
flow.plain: The screen calls the submit hook from the one data layer. That fires off a POST request to the backend to submit the commit, and attaches the user's login token to the request so the server knows who is asking.
flow.deep: The hook runs through the single RTK Query slice `commitApi` (`libs/api/src/commitApi.ts`), which issues `POST /api/commits/{id}/submit`. `baseQuery.prepareHeaders` (`commitApi.ts:86-98`) attaches `Authorization: Bearer <token>` from the injectable `tokenProvider` (or `X-Debug-Member` in the hermetic E2E path). On success its `invalidatesTags` busts `commitTag(id)` + `weekListTag()` so the week list and that commit refetch automatically (`commitApi.ts:145-149`).

flow.title: Request leaves the remote inside the host's router/proxy
flow.plain: That request leaves the Weekly Commit remote, which is running embedded inside the host shell. During development the host forwards anything starting with /api to the backend server.
flow.deep: The remote is mounted inside the host's `BrowserRouter` with `react`/`react-dom`/`react-router-dom` shared as MF singletons (`apps/host-shell/vite.config.ts:32-38`); the request to `/api` (same-origin) is proxied by the host's Vite dev server to `WCM_API_TARGET ?? :8080` (`apps/host-shell/vite.config.ts:45-67`). In production the same `/api` path is fronted by CloudFront to the EKS backend.

flow.title: JWT validated; member resolved from the subject
flow.plain: At the backend, the security filter checks the login token is genuine. Then the service works out who is acting from the token itself — never from anything in the request body — so you can only lock your own week.
flow.deep: The `@Profile("!e2e")` `SecurityFilterChain` validates the Auth0 RS256 JWT (signature against the JWKS, issuer/expiry, and audience when set). Since `POST .../submit` isn't manager/admin-gated it only requires `authenticated` (`SecurityConfig.java:97`). The service then resolves the acting member via `CurrentMemberProvider` from the **JWT subject** (`JwtCurrentMemberProvider.java:35`), never a client-supplied id — the structural IDOR/BOLA defense — and row-level ownership 403s anyone but the owner.

flow.title: LifecycleService.lock: guard, freeze snapshot, emit event
flow.plain: The core rulebook checks the move from draft to locked is allowed (at least one item, every item linked), takes a permanent frozen copy of the plan, stamps the submission time, and raises an internal commit.locked event. An illegal move is rejected with a clear 409 conflict.
flow.deep: `CommitService.submit` hydrates the items, rejects a zero-item commit (422) and any unlinked item (422), then calls `lifecycle.lock(...)` (`LifecycleService.java:39`). The guard re-checks non-empty + every-item-linked (`:41-:54`), freezes an immutable `CommitSnapshot` of every item's text/link/tier (`:55-:59`), sets `LOCKED`, stamps `submittedAt`, and the service publishes `commit.locked` (`CommitService.java:155-159`). An un-enumerated transition is illegal by construction (`LifecycleTransition.java:49`) → `IllegalTransitionException` → 409.

flow.title: Return CommitDto / ProblemDetail; RTK invalidates tags
flow.plain: The controller hands back the updated commit as JSON; if anything went wrong it comes back in the one standard error shape. Back on the frontend, the data layer's cache rules then refetch the week list automatically.
flow.deep: `CommitController.submit` forwards with no logic of its own and serializes the updated `CommitDto`; on a guard failure `ApiExceptionHandler` (`ApiExceptionHandler.java:39`) renders an RFC-7807 `ProblemDetail` with the right status and a stable `code` the FE branches on. On the client, `submitCommit`'s `invalidatesTags` (`commitApi.ts:145-149`) evicts the matching cache entries, so any mounted subscriber to the week list and that commit refetches.

flow.title: commit.locked → consumer → Outlook
flow.plain: The commit.locked event drives a consumer that asks the calendar port to create an Outlook event for the week. If Microsoft is down, that failure does not undo the lock — the calendar entry is a side effect, not part of the core transaction.
flow.deep: After the LOCK transaction commits, `EventPublisher.publish(commit.locked)` fires; by default `InProcessEventPublisher` re-publishes on the Spring context and `CommitLockedCalendarConsumer` (`CommitLockedCalendarConsumer.java:29`) runs `syncOnce` — dedup on `eventId`, check the `createEventOnLock` preference, then `CalendarSyncPort.syncLockedCommit`, which under `@Profile("graph")` POSTs a real delegated `/me/events` (`GraphCalendarAdapter.java:31`). A Graph error is caught and logged **non-fatally** (`CommitLockedCalendarConsumer.java:58-60`); under `aws`, the same event instead flows SNS → SQS, where failures propagate so SQS redrives to the DLQ.

glossary.plain: The four-level strategy tree that company goals live in: Rally Cry, then Defining Objective, then Outcome, then Supporting Outcome.
The top of the strategy tree — the big annual theme everything else rolls up to.
The second strategy level: a concrete objective that supports the year's Rally Cry.
The third strategy level: a measurable result that supports a Defining Objective.
The bottom, linkable level of the strategy tree — the specific thing a week's work item is attached to.
A priority label for each item using chess pieces, from King (most important) down to Pawn (smallest).
One person's plan for one week: a list of items, each tied to a piece of strategy, that moves through the lifecycle.
The rulebook that moves a week through its fixed states — Draft, Locked, Reconciling, Reconciled, Carry-forward — only allowing legal moves.
After the week, comparing what you planned (the frozen snapshot) against what actually happened (the live status of each item).
A permanent frozen copy of the plan taken at the moment you lock the week, so planned-vs-actual stays trustworthy.
Copying the items you didn't finish into a fresh draft for next week.
Splitting the UI into separate apps that join up at runtime, so the feature can be loaded into a host page live rather than bundled in.
The remote is the app that produces the feature (wc-remote); the host is the thin shell that loads and displays it.
The one data tool the frontend uses to call the backend and cache results, so screens never make raw network calls.
Labels on cached data so that when something changes, exactly the right screens refetch themselves automatically.
Checking not just that you may reach a route, but that you may touch this specific record — your own week, your own reports.
Auth0 is the login service; a JWT is the signed token it issues that proves who you are on every request.
Microsoft Graph, the API used to create a calendar event in a user's Outlook when they lock a week.
An optional AWS path where internal events travel through a topic (SNS) and a queue (SQS) instead of staying in-process.
A shared base for the data records that auto-fills who created or changed a row and when.
A versioned database change script (here V1 through V10) that owns the schema the app validates against at startup.
A full in-memory fake backend that mirrors the real API, so the frontend can build, test, and run end-to-end with no live server.
